content:
# John Chapter 21 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JHN_021_page_001.jpg

1. After these things the Yeshua manifested himself again to the disciples at the sea of the Tiberias. And he manifested [himself] thus.

2. There were together Simon Petros and Thomas the [one] called Didymos and Nathanael the [one] from Kana of the Galilaia and the [sons] of Zebedaios and two others of the disciples of him.

3. Simon Petros says to them, "I go to fish." They say to him, "We also come with you." They went out and embarked into the boat, and on that night they caught nothing.

4. And early [morning] already having come, the Yeshua stood on the shore; yet the disciples did not know that it is the Yeshua.

5. Then the Yeshua says to them, "Children, you do not have any food, do you?" They answered him, "No."

6. And he said to them, "Cast the net to the right side of the boat, and you will find [fish]." Then they cast, and no longer were they able to draw it because of the multitude of the fish.

7. Then that disciple whom the Yeshua loved says to Petros, "It is the Lord!" Then Simon Petros, having heard that it is the Lord, tied the outer garment around [himself] — for he was stripped — and threw himself into the sea.

8. But the other disciples came in the small boat — for they were not far from the land, but about two hundred cubits — dragging the net of the fish.

9. When therefore they went ashore, they see a charcoal fire set, and fish lying on [it], and bread.

10. The Yeshua says to them, "Bring [some] of the fish that you caught now."

11. Then Simon Petros went up and drew the net to the land, full of large fish, a hundred fifty-three; and though being so many, the net was not torn.

12. The Yeshua says to them, "Come, eat breakfast." But none of the disciples dared to ask him, "Who are you?" — knowing that it is the Lord.

13. The Yeshua comes and takes the bread and gives [it] to them, and the fish likewise.

14. This already [was the] third [time] the Yeshua was manifested to the disciples, having been raised from [the] dead.

15. When therefore they ate breakfast, the Yeshua says to Simon Petros, "Simon [son] of Ioannes, do you love me more than these?" He says to him, "Yes, Lord, you know that I love you." He says to him, "Feed the lambs of me."

16. He says to him again a second [time], "Simon [son] of Ioannes, do you love me?" He says to him, "Yes, Lord, you know that I love you." He says to him, "Shepherd the sheep of me."

17. He says to him the third [time], "Simon [son] of Ioannes, do you love me?" Petros was grieved because he said to him the third [time], "Do you love me?" And he said to him, "Lord, you know all things; you know that I love you." The Yeshua says to him, "Feed the sheep of me.

18. Amen, amen, I say to you, when you were younger, you girded yourself and walked where you wished; but when you grow old, you will stretch out the hands of you, and another will gird you and carry [you] where you do not wish."

19. And this he said signifying by what kind of death he will glorify the God. And having said this, he says to him, "Follow me."

20. Petros, having turned, sees the disciple whom the Yeshua loved following — who also reclined at the supper upon the breast of him and said, "Lord, who is the [one] betraying you?" —

21. This [one] therefore Petros having seen says to the Yeshua, "Lord, and this [one] — what?"

22. The Yeshua says to him, "If I wish him to remain until I come, what [is it] to you? You follow me."

23. Then this word went out to the brothers, that that disciple does not die. But the Yeshua did not say to him that he does not die, but, "If I wish him to remain until I come, what [is it] to you?"

24. This is the disciple testifying concerning these things and having written these things, and we know that the testimony of him is true.

25. And there are also many other things that the Yeshua did, which if they were written one by one, I suppose not even the world itself would have room for the books being written.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.9 — "A charcoal fire" (ἀνθρακιάν):** This word appears only twice in the NT — here and in 18:18, where Peter denied Jesus beside a charcoal fire. The deliberate echo creates a literary bridge: Peter denied Jesus by a charcoal fire; he is restored by a charcoal fire. The setting reverses the scene of failure.

2. **v.11 — "A hundred fifty-three":** The specific number has generated centuries of speculation. Jerome connected it to ancient zoological claims about 153 species of fish (a universal catch). Others note it is the triangular number of 17 (1+2+3...+17=153). Most likely, it is simply an eyewitness detail that stuck in memory.

3. **v.15-17 — The love vocabulary:** The first two questions use ἀγαπᾷς ("do you love me?" — ἀγαπάω). Peter's replies use φιλῶ ("I love you" — φιλέω). In the third question, Jesus switches to φιλεῖς — descending to Peter's own word. Whether this shift is theologically significant or merely stylistic variation is debated. John uses the two verbs interchangeably elsewhere (cf. 3:35 vs. 5:20).

4. **v.18-19 — Peter's death:** "You will stretch out your hands" — traditionally understood as a reference to crucifixion. Church tradition (Tertullian, Eusebius) records that Peter was crucified in Rome under Nero, approximately 64-68 CE. "By what kind of death he would glorify God" — in John's theology, martyrdom is glorification.

5. **v.24 — "This is the disciple... who has written these things":** The Beloved Disciple is identified as the author (or authority behind) the Gospel. "We know" — a community voice authenticating the testimony. This suggests a community of disciples who know and vouch for the author.

— EMET, AI Translator
